topic: The Changing Landscapes of the UK
subject: geography

items.q: Why do some rivers form deltas and others form estuaries?
items.correct: Deltas form where rivers deposit sediment faster than the sea can remove it (calm seas, heavy load); estuaries form where tidal energy removes sediment (strong tides).
items.misconception: Deltas form on big rivers and estuaries on small ones.
items.random_wrong: Deltas form in cold climates and estuaries in warm climates.
items.why: Size doesn't determine the form. The balance of sediment supply vs marine energy does — the Nile (delta) and Thames (estuary) are similar size but in different sea conditions.

items.q: Why are tropical rainforest soils typically infertile?
items.correct: Heavy rainfall leaches nutrients, and most nutrients are stored in living biomass and rapidly recycled — the soil itself holds very little.
items.misconception: Because so much vegetation grows there, the soil must be fertile.
items.random_wrong: Because rainforest soils are made of clay.
items.why: The lush vegetation hides poor soil. The rainforest works as a tightly closed nutrient cycle; once cleared, the system collapses quickly because the soil cannot sustain growth.

items.q: Why is the urban heat island effect strongest at night?
items.correct: Concrete and brick released their stored daytime heat slowly into the night air, while rural areas cool rapidly through radiative loss — widening the urban-rural temperature gap after sunset.
items.misconception: Urban heat islands are strongest at midday because cities are sunniest then.
items.random_wrong: Urban heat islands only happen in summer.
items.why: Thermal mass of urban materials is the key — they buffer city temperature. At night, this buffering creates the largest urban-rural difference, sometimes 5-10 degrees C in major cities.

items.q: Which line divides upland and lowland Britain?
items.why: Correct answer: Tees-Exe Line. “Watford Line” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Where are softer sedimentary rocks found in UK?
items.why: It's Southeast England. “Scottish Highlands” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately how many km of coastline does the UK have?
items.why: The real figure is 17820 km. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
